This is a major operation that changes issue IDs and should have strong
confirmation with clear warnings about the impact on references and git
history.

description:
Refactor the beads-stats command to use the beads-statistics class and make the output interactive.

**Current State:**
- beads-stats.el displays statistics using ad-hoc parsing
- Output is static, non-interactive
- Uses simple text rendering

**Desired State:**
- Use beads-statistics class and beads-statistics-from-json parser
- Make numbers clickable/interactive using button.el
- Clicking on stats (Total, Open, In Progress, Closed, Blocked, Ready) opens beads-list filtered to those issues
- Output should look identical to 'bd stats' (non-JSON) CLI output
- Maintain keyboard navigation (g to refresh, q to quit)

**Design Approach:**
1. Replace ad-hoc parsing with beads-statistics-from-json
2. Use button.el or text properties to make numbers clickable
3. Add click handlers that call beads-list with appropriate filters:
   - Total: beads-list (no filter)
   - Open: beads-list --status open
   - In Progress: beads-list --status in_progress
   - Closed: beads-list --status closed
   - Blocked: beads-blocked (or beads-list with blocked filter)
   - Ready: beads-ready
4. Preserve existing visual format and color scheme

**Testing:**
- Unit tests for button creation
- Integration tests for click actions
- Verify visual output matches CLI
- Test all filter combinations work correctly
notes:
Core implementation complete (4/6 tasks done):
✓ beads.el-guj: Refactored to use beads-statistics class
✓ beads.el-fnq: Added button infrastructure using button.el
✓ beads.el-8qb: Wired up button actions to beads-list commands
✓ beads.el-1s8: Updated rendering with clickable buttons

Remaining work:
- beads.el-4xv: Add comprehensive tests for interactive features
- beads.el-56r: Update documentation (README.md, docstrings)

All quality gates passing:
- Tests: 31/31 ✓
- Lint: No warnings ✓  
- Compile: Successful ✓

The interactive stats feature is now functional. Users can click on any statistic to view filtered issue lists.
title: Refactor beads-stats to be interactive

description:
When pressing n/p in beads views (list, show, etc.), point should move to the next/previous clickable element.

Additionally, when a beads view buffer is opened, point should initially be positioned on the first clickable element rather than at the beginning of the buffer.
notes:
After investigation, this feature requires several design decisions:

**Current State:**
- beads-list: n/p moves forward-line (no buttons)
- beads-show: n/p navigates sections; TAB/[/] already navigate buttons
- beads-stats: n/p not bound; has clickable stat buttons

**Design Questions:**
1. Should n/p REPLACE existing behavior or be NEW keys?
   - beads-show uses n/p for section nav - breaking change?
   - Should we use different keys like C-n/C-p for buttons?

2. How to handle mixed content (show view)?
   - Buttons are WITHIN sections
   - Do we want n/p for buttons AND keep section nav?
   - Perhaps M-n/M-p for sections, n/p for buttons?

3. Initial positioning feature needs design:
   - When should point jump to first button?
   - On buffer creation? After refresh?
   - Could be jarring if user is reading from top

**Recommendation:**
This needs user input on desired behavior before implementation. The current button navigation in beads-show (TAB, [, ]) works well. We should decide if n/p should:
- Option A: Keep current (sections in show, lines in list)
- Option B: Change to buttons (breaking change for show)
- Option C: Context-sensitive (buttons if any, else fallback)

Marking as blocked pending design decision.
title: Improve n/p navigation to move to next/previous clickable element

description:
Implement Emacs interface for the 'bd sync' command.

The bd sync command synchronizes issues with git remote in a single operation:
1. Export pending changes to JSONL
2. Commit changes to git
3. Pull from remote (with conflict resolution)
4. Import updated JSONL
5. Push local commits to remote

**Features:**
- One-command git sync workflow
- Dry-run preview
- Custom commit message
- Skip pull or push (--no-pull, --no-push)
- Display sync progress and results

**Implementation:**
- Add to beads-misc.el or create beads-sync.el
- Transient menu with flags:
  - --dry-run: Preview without making changes
  - -m/--message: Custom commit message
  - --no-pull: Skip pulling from remote
  - --no-push: Skip pushing to remote
- Display progress in steps
- Show sync results (commits pulled/pushed)
- Error handling for conflicts
- Refresh all buffers after sync
title: beads.el: Implement bd sync command

description:
Implement Emacs interface for the 'bd delete' command.

The bd delete command permanently deletes an issue and cleans up all references to it. This is a destructive operation that:
1. Removes all dependency links (any type, both directions)
2. Updates text references to '[deleted:ID]' in directly connected issues
3. Deletes the issue from the database

**Implementation:**
- Add to beads-misc.el or create beads-delete.el
- Transient menu with flags:
  - -f/--force: Actually delete (without this, shows preview)
- Preview mode shows:
  - Issue details
  - All dependencies that will be removed
  - All text references that will be updated
- Confirmation prompt with detailed warning
- Display results after deletion
- Context-aware: can delete current issue from show/list buffer
title: beads.el: Implement bd delete command

description:
Update the beads-agent-session EIEIO class to use project directory as identity instead of issue ID.

## Key Change
**Directory is identity, issue is current-focus.**

The session's primary key is now `project-dir`, not `issue-id`.

## File to Modify
`lisp/beads-agent-backend.el`

## New Class (Directory-Bound)
```elisp
(defclass beads-agent-session ()
  ((id
    :initarg :id
    :type string
    :documentation "Session ID: project-name#N (e.g., beads.el#1)")
   (project-dir
    :initarg :project-dir
    :type string
    :documentation "Project directory (THE IDENTITY)")
   (project-name
    :initarg :project-name
    :type string
    :documentation "Short name for display/session ID")
   (instance-number
    :initarg :instance-number
    :type integer
    :documentation "Instance N for this project (1, 2, 3...)")
   (current-issue
    :initarg :current-issue
    :initform nil
    :documentation "Current focus issue ID (can change mid-session)")
   (touched-issues
    :initarg :touched-issues
    :initform nil
    :type list
    :documentation "List of issue IDs worked on this session")
   ;; Existing slots preserved
   (backend-name ...)
   (agent-type-name ...)
   (started-at ...)
   (backend-session ...)
   (buffer ...)))
```

## Slot Changes Summary
| Slot | Change |
|------|--------|
| `id` | Now `project-name#N` format |
| `issue-id` | REMOVED (was identity) |
| `project-dir` | NEW (THE identity) |
| `project-name` | NEW (for display) |
| `instance-number` | NEW (explicit slot) |
| `current-issue` | NEW (focus, can change) |
| `touched-issues` | NEW (tracking) |

## Accessor Functions to Add
```elisp
(defun beads-agent-session-add-touched-issue (session issue-id)
  "Add ISSUE-ID to SESSION's touched-issues if not present.")

(defun beads-agent-session-set-current-issue (session issue-id)
  "Set SESSION's current focus to ISSUE-ID, add to touched.")
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All new slots defined with proper types
- [ ] `project-dir` documented as THE IDENTITY
- [ ] `current-issue` documented as changeable focus
- [ ] Accessor functions for touched-issues tracking
- [ ] Unit tests for class and accessors
- [ ] No byte-compilation warnings
title: Update beads-agent-session EIEIO class for directory identity

description:
When stopping agents via the beads-agent-issue management menu (k or K commands), the stop operation fails with 'Text is read-only' error in the process sentinel.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start an agent on an issue using agent-shell backend
2. Press A on the same issue to open management menu
3. Press k (stop one) or K (stop all)
4. Confirm with y

## Expected
Agent stops and is removed from session list

## Actual
Error in *Messages*:
- 'error in process sentinel: delete-process: Text is read-only'
- 'acp-shutdown: Client already shut down'

Agent sessions remain active despite confirmation.

## Environment
- Emacs 30.2
- beads.el from ai-agent branch
- agent-shell backend with Claude Code agent

## Analysis
The error suggests the agent buffer is read-only when the process sentinel tries to clean up. This may be an issue with how agent-shell backend handles the stop operation, or a timing issue with buffer state.
title: Agent stop fails with 'Text is read-only' error

description:
Implement Emacs interface for the 'bd restore' command.

The bd restore command restores full history of a compacted issue from git version control. When an issue is compacted, the git commit hash is saved. This command:
1. Reads the compacted_at_commit from the database
2. Checks out that commit temporarily
3. Reads the full issue from JSONL at that point in history
4. Displays the full issue history
5. Returns to the current git state

This is read-only and does not modify database or git state.

**Implementation:**
- Add to beads-compact.el or beads-misc.el
- Simple command: beads-restore ISSUE-ID
- Display restored issue in special buffer
- Show full description, events, history
- Indicate it's a historical view
- Context-aware: can restore current compacted issue
title: beads.el: Implement bd restore command

description:
## Location
`lisp/beads-sesman.el:202-205` - `beads-sesman--register-session`

## Problem
Buffer may be killed between `beads-agent-backend-get-buffer` and `sesman-link-session`:

```elisp
(when-let* ((backend (beads-agent--get-backend (oref beads-session backend-name)))
            (buffer (beads-agent-backend-get-buffer backend beads-session)))
  (when (buffer-live-p buffer)
    ;; WINDOW: buffer could be killed here before next line!
    (sesman-link-session beads-sesman-system sesman-session 'buffer buffer)))
```

If buffer is killed after `beads-agent-backend-get-buffer` but before line 205, we'll try to link a dead buffer to sesman.

## Impact
Potential error or undefined behavior when linking dead buffer.

## Fix
Re-check `buffer-live-p` immediately before linking, or use atomic check-and-link pattern.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No use-after-free possible
- [ ] Graceful handling of buffer killed during registration
- [ ] Excellent test coverage
- [ ] Tested in live Emacs session inside tmux
title: Fix use-after-free in sesman registration

title: Multi-Agent Worktree Support

description:
When creating an issue via beads-create, it fails with:
"Wrong type argument: (or eieio-object cl-structure-object oclosure), nil"

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Run M-x beads-create
2. Fill in all supported issue fields
3. Execute the create command

**Root Cause Analysis:**
The error occurs when `beads-command-validate` is called with nil at line 97 of beads-create.el. This is a generic method dispatch error - EIEIO cannot dispatch on nil.

The call chain is:
1. `beads-create--execute` calls `transient-args 'beads-create`
2. Passes result to `beads-create--parse-transient-args`
3. Calls `beads-command-validate` on the result
4. If result is nil, generic method dispatch fails

While `beads-create--parse-transient-args` normally returns a valid object (even with nil args), there may be edge cases causing nil return:
- Corrupted transient context
- Silent parsing error
- Runtime conditions not reproduced in testing

**Fix approach:**
Add defensive nil checking before calling generic methods, with user-friendly error messages.
notes:
Plan created 2025-12-30.

Key changes:
- Add defensive nil checks before generic method calls
- Provide user-friendly error messages instead of cryptic type errors
- Add regression test to prevent this issue from recurring

Investigation approach taken:
- Traced error to beads-command-validate being called with nil
- Verified beads-create--parse-transient-args works correctly in isolation
- Issue may be environment-specific or related to transient context state
- Fix adds defensive programming regardless of exact trigger condition
title: Fix beads-create: Wrong type argument error on issue creation

description:
Migrate all bd command execution from the legacy beads--run-command function to use EIEIO command classes defined in beads-command.el.

## Current State

The codebase has 141 occurrences of beads--run-command across 21 files. This legacy approach bypasses the benefits of:
- Type safety and validation
- Consistent command-line building
- Better testability
- Unified error handling
- Timing and logging instrumentation

## Goal

Replace all beads--run-command calls with equivalent beads-command-* class usage, providing full 1-to-1 coverage of bd CLI commands.

## Command Classes Status

**Already exist:**
- beads-command-init
- beads-command-list
- beads-command-create
- beads-command-epic-status
- beads-command-epic-close-eligible

**Need to create:**
- beads-command-show
- beads-command-update
- beads-command-close
- beads-command-ready
- beads-command-blocked
- beads-command-stats
- beads-command-dep-add
- beads-command-dep-remove
- beads-command-dep-tree
- beads-command-dep-cycles

## Implementation Strategy

1. Create missing command classes in beads-command.el (following beads.el-10i pattern)
2. Add integration tests for each new command class
3. Migrate modules one at a time, updating tests as we go
4. Remove beads--run-command and beads--run-command-async once all usage is eliminated

## Benefits

- Consistent API across all bd commands
- Better error messages and validation
- Performance monitoring via timing instrumentation
- Easier to maintain and extend
- Type-safe command construction
notes: Completed test fixes for EIEIO migration. All 1087 tests now pass (100% pass rate). Fixed test mocks in beads-types-test.el, beads-list-test.el, and beads-show-test.el to properly convert alists to EIEIO objects and use correct function signatures.

description:
Refactor beads-command-to-args to beads-command-line with improved semantics:

Current behavior:
  (beads-command-to-args (beads-command-list)) => ("list" "--json")

Desired behavior:
  (beads-command-line (beads-command-list)) => ("bd" "list" "--json")

Changes required:
1. Rename function: beads-command-to-args -> beads-command-line
2. Update implementation to prepend beads-executable (or "bd")
3. Update all callers to handle full command line instead of just args
4. Update all tests to reflect the new behavior
5. Run full quality checks (test, lint, compile)

This will make the function name more accurate (it returns a command line, not just args) and simplify some call sites that currently need to manually prepend the executable.
notes:
## Implementation Plan

### Analysis
After examining the codebase:

**Current Usage:**
- `beads-command-to-args` is a generic method with implementations for:
  - beads-command (base class - global flags)
  - beads-command-json (adds --json flag)
  - beads-command-init (adds init-specific flags)
  - beads-command-list (adds list-specific flags)
  - beads-command-create (adds create-specific flags)

- **Only caller:** `beads-command-execute` at line 199:
  ```elisp
  (let* ((args (beads-command-to-args command))
         (cmd (cons beads-executable args))
  ```

- **No direct tests:** Tests only verify end-to-end behavior via beads-command-execute

### Implementation Options

**Option 1: Simple Rename + Update Base Method** (RECOMMENDED)
- Rename generic + all methods: beads-command-to-args → beads-command-line
- Update base method (beads-command) to prepend executable
- Update caller in beads-command-execute (line 199)
- Update commentary/docstrings (lines 23, 36, 144-147)
- No test changes needed (integration tests don't directly test this function)

Pros: Simple, minimal changes, clear semantics
Cons: None

**Option 2: Keep Old Name + Add New Function**
- Keep beads-command-to-args as-is
- Add new beads-command-line that calls beads-command-to-args
- Gradually deprecate old function

Pros: Backward compatible (unnecessary - internal API)
Cons: More code, confusing to have both

**Option 3: Add Parameter to Control Behavior**
- Rename to beads-command-line
- Add optional parameter to control whether to include executable

Pros: Flexible
Cons: Overengineered for single use case

### Recommended Approach: Option 1

**Implementation Steps:**
1. Update generic definition (line 144)
2. Update all method implementations (lines 155, 252, 337, 550, 790)
3. Update caller in beads-command-execute (line 199) - remove cons operation
4. Update commentary and docstrings (lines 23, 36, 144-147)
5. Run tests: guix shell -D -f guix.scm -- eldev -p -dtT test beads-command-test
6. Run lint: guix shell -D -f guix.scm -- eldev -p -dtT lint
7. Run compile: guix shell -D -f guix.scm -- eldev -p -dtT compile

**Files to modify:**
- lisp/beads-command.el (only file)

**Expected changes:**
- Rename function in 6 places (1 generic + 5 methods)
- Update 1 caller (line 199)
- Update ~5 docstrings/comments
- Total: ~15 lines changed

**Risk assessment:** LOW
- Internal API only (not exposed to users)
- Single call site
- No test changes needed (tests verify behavior, not implementation)
title: Rename beads-command-to-args to beads-command-line and return full command including executable

description:
Agent buffer naming should use project-name (directory identity) but also include the agent type name for clarity.

## Current (wrong)
`*beads-agent[test-8hb][Plan#1]*` - uses issue-id instead of project-name

## Expected
`*beads-agent[beads.el][Plan#1]*` - uses project-name + agent-type

## Format
`*beads-agent[PROJECT-NAME][TYPE#N]*`

Where:
- PROJECT-NAME: from project directory (identity)
- TYPE: agent type name (Plan, Task, Review, QA, Custom)
- N: instance number

## Why include agent type?
- Helps identify what the agent is doing at a glance
- Multiple agent types can run in same project
- Distinguishes Plan (read-only) from Task (modifying) agents

## Files to modify
- `beads-agent-backend.el`: `beads-agent--generate-buffer-name`
- Ensure `proj-name` slot is populated when creating sessions

## Related
- Part of epic beads.el-33c9 directory-aware buffer system
- Found during testing - session shows `proj-name: nil`

description:
Review and standardize key bindings throughout beads.el for consistency
and alignment with popular Emacs modes (Magit, dired, ibuffer, etc.).

## Current Issues

### Inconsistent Buffer-Level Keys
- Close: `d` or `k` in list mode
- Reopen: `o` in list mode
- Create: `c` or `+` in list mode
- Update: `e` in list mode
- Delete: `D` in list mode

Some of these conflict with common Emacs conventions or are inconsistent
with each other.

### Inconsistent Transient Menu Execute Keys
Different transient menus use different keys for the main action:
- beads-create: `x` to execute
- beads-update: `x` to execute
- beads-close: `x` to execute
- beads-reopen: `x` to execute
- Other menus may vary

We should standardize on one key (probably `x` or `RET`) across all
transient menus.

## Research Conventions

Study key bindings from popular modes:
- **Magit**: How does it handle similar operations?
- **dired**: File operations (delete, rename, etc.)
- **ibuffer**: Buffer operations (mark, delete, etc.)
- **org-mode**: Task state changes
- **package-menu-mode**: Install/delete operations

## Proposed Changes

### Buffer-Level Keys
Consider these conventions:
- `RET` - Show/view issue details (current)
- `c` - Create new issue (current, matches dired `C` for copy/create)
- `e` - Edit/update issue (current, matches dired edit)
- `d` - Mark for deletion/closing (like dired `d` for delete)
- `x` - Execute marked operations (like dired `x`)
- `k` - Kill/close immediately (like `k` in many modes)
- `o` - Reopen (current)
- `D` - Delete permanently (current, matches dired `D`)

Or alternative:
- `c` - Close issue (more intuitive than `d`?)
- `r` - Reopen issue (instead of `o`?)

### Transient Menu Keys
Standardize execute key across all transient menus:
- Primary action: `x` (execute) or `RET` or `C-c C-c`
- Preview: `P` or `C-c C-p`
- Reset: `R` or `C-c C-k`
- Quit: `q` or `C-c C-q`

### Bulk Operations
- `B` prefix for bulk operations (current)
- Consistent with buffer-level operations where possible

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Document current key bindings across all modes
- [ ] Research conventions from popular Emacs modes
- [ ] Propose consistent key binding scheme
- [ ] Get user feedback on proposal
- [ ] Update all key bindings consistently
- [ ] Update documentation (README, docstrings)
- [ ] Update tests for new key bindings
- [ ] Verify no conflicts with standard Emacs keys
- [ ] All existing functionality still accessible

## Notes

This is a breaking change for existing users, so:
- Consider adding a compatibility layer or migration guide
- Document changes clearly in CHANGELOG
- Consider making key bindings customizable
- Test thoroughly to ensure no regressions
notes:
Additional insights from discussion:

## RET vs x for Execute

RET (Return/Enter) might be better than `x` for executing commands because:
- More intuitive - "press Enter to do it"
- Standard in many Emacs interfaces (completing-read, etc.)
- One less keystroke (no Shift needed)
- Muscle memory from other applications

If RET works, consider using it as primary execute key with `x` as alternative.

## Main Menu vs Bulk Operations Consistency

KEY INSIGHT: Bindings should be consistent between:
1. Main transient menu (M-x beads)
2. Buffer-level single operations (e.g., `c` in list mode)
3. Bulk operations (e.g., `B c` in list mode)

Example inconsistency to fix:
- If main menu uses `c` for create
- And list mode uses `c` for create
- And bulk prefix is `B`
- Then bulk operations should mirror: `B c`, `B e`, `B d`, etc.

Current state to check:
- Main menu: What keys are used?
- List mode single: `c` create, `e` update, `d`/`k` close, `o` reopen, `D` delete
- List mode bulk: `B s` (status), `B p` (priority), `B c` (close), `B o` (reopen)

Notice: Bulk uses different keys than single operations!
- Single: `e` for update
- Bulk: `B s` for status, `B p` for priority (no `B e` for update?)

Proposal: Make bulk operations use same keys as single operations
- `B c` - bulk close (matches `d`/`k` single close)
- `B o` - bulk reopen (matches `o` single reopen)  
- `B e` - bulk update (matches `e` single update)
- `B d` - bulk delete? (matches `D` single delete)

This makes it easier to remember: "press B before the normal key to do bulk operation"
title: Revisit and standardize key bindings across beads.el

description:
Migrate beads-command-create class to use slot metadata as proof of concept.

## Goal

Demonstrate the full meta-programming system by converting beads-command-create
to use slot properties instead of manual boilerplate.

## Before (current)

```elisp
;; In beads-command.el - slot definition
(title
 :initarg :title
 :type (or null string)
 :initform nil
 :documentation "Issue title")

;; In beads-command.el - manual command-line builder (~50 lines)
(cl-defmethod beads-command-line ((command beads-command-create))
  (with-slots (title ...) command
    (let ((args (list "create")))
      (when title (setq args (append args (list title))))
      ...)))

;; In beads-option.el - manual infix definition
(transient-define-infix beads-option-issue-title ()
  :class 'transient-option
  :key "t"
  :description "Title (required)"
  :argument "--title="
  :reader #'beads-reader-issue-title)

;; In beads-create.el - manual parse function (~40 lines)
(defun beads-create--parse-transient-args (args)
  (let* ((title (transient-arg-value "--title=" args))
         ...)
    (beads-command-create :title title ...)))
```

## After (with metadata)

```elisp
;; In beads-command.el - enriched slot definition (single source of truth)
(title
 :initarg :title
 :type (or null string)
 :initform nil
 :documentation "Issue title"
 ;; CLI properties
 :positional 1            ; First positional argument
 ;; Transient properties
 :transient-key "t"
 :transient-description "Title (required)"
 :transient-class transient-option
 :transient-reader beads-reader-issue-title
 :transient-group "Required"
 :transient-level 1
 :transient-order 1
 ;; Validation
 :required t)

;; Command-line builder uses metadata automatically
;; Transient infixes generated automatically
;; Parse function generated automatically
```

## Migration Steps

1. Add slot properties to all beads-command-create slots
2. Verify beads-meta-build-command-line produces same output
3. Verify generated infixes match existing ones
4. Verify generated parse function produces same results
5. Remove manual beads-command-line method (use meta version)
6. Remove manual infix definitions from beads-option.el
7. Remove manual parse function from beads-create.el
8. Update beads-create.el to use generated prefix (or verify it works)
9. Run all tests to ensure no regressions

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All beads-command-create slots have metadata
- [ ] Command-line output identical to before
- [ ] Transient menu looks and works identical
- [ ] All existing tests pass
- [ ] Code reduction measured (lines removed)
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] Lint and compile pass
title: Migrate beads-command-create to use slot metadata (proof of concept)

description:
Integrate label display and management into beads-list and beads-show buffers.

## Overview

Enhance existing beads-list and beads-show buffers to display issue labels and provide convenient keybindings for label operations.

## Features for beads-show.el

1. Display labels in issue detail view:
   - Add "Labels:" section showing all labels for the issue
   - Format: comma-separated, with color/highlighting

2. Add keybindings:
   - 'l a': Add label to current issue (calls beads-label-add)
   - 'l r': Remove label from current issue (calls beads-label-remove)
   - 'l l': List labels for current issue (calls beads-label-list)

## Features for beads-list.el

2. Add keybindings:
   - 'l a': Add label to current/marked issue(s)
   - 'l r': Remove label from current/marked issue(s)
   - 'l l': List labels for current issue
   - 'l L': List all labels

3. Support batch operations:
   - When multiple issues are marked, operate on all marked issues

## Implementation

2. Modify beads-list.el:

   - Add label keybindings to beads-list-mode-map
   - Support batch operations via marked issues

3. Helper functions:

## Acceptance Criteria

description:
Implement automatic command-line argument building from slot metadata.

## Implementation

Create `beads-meta-build-command-line` function that:
1. Iterates through all slots of a command instance
2. Reads `:long-option`, `:short-option`, `:option-type`, `:positional` properties
3. Builds CLI arguments automatically based on slot values and metadata

## Slot Type Handling

- `:string` - Append as `--option value`
- `:boolean` - Append as `--flag` when truthy
- `:integer` - Convert to string, append as `--option value`
- `:list` - Join with separator (`:option-separator`, default ","), append as `--option val1,val2`

## Positional Arguments

`:positional` is an integer indicating position (1, 2, 3...):

```elisp
;; Slot with :positional 1, value "My Issue"
;; → Placed first in command line

;; Slot with :positional 2, :option-type :list, value '("bd-1" "bd-2")
;; → ("bd-1" "bd-2") placed second
```

Positional args are sorted by their `:positional` value, then placed before or after named options as appropriate for the command.

## Named Options

Slots without `:positional` (nil) use `:long-option` or `:short-option`:

```elisp
;; Slot with :long-option "--priority", value 1
;; → ("--priority" "1")

;; Slot with :option-type :boolean, :long-option "--force", value t
;; → ("--force")
```

## Integration

1. Create `beads-command-line-from-meta` generic method
2. Can be used as default implementation or called by custom methods
3. Should handle missing metadata gracefully (skip slots without :long-option or :positional)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `beads-meta-build-command-line` function implemented
- [ ] All option types handled correctly
- [ ] Positional arguments (integer) sorted and placed correctly
- [ ] Named options work with :long-option/:short-option
- [ ] Graceful handling of slots without metadata
- [ ] ERT tests covering all cases
- [ ] Lint and compile pass
title: Implement automatic command-line builder from slot metadata

close_reason: EIEIO meta-programming migration complete - all commands now use slot metadata
description:
Leverage EIEIO introspection to reduce boilerplate code in beads.el by adding custom slot properties to command classes.

## Problem

Currently there is significant code duplication:
1. **beads-command-line methods** (~900 lines) - Every command class manually builds CLI args from slots
2. **--parse-transient-args functions** (~12 modules) - Manually map transient args to command slots
3. **transient-define-infix definitions** (~60 in beads-option.el) - Duplicate slot metadata
4. **Validation functions** - Repeated patterns across modules

## Solution

Use closql-style approach to add custom slot properties to EIEIO classes:

1. Define custom slot properties via `define-advice` on `eieio-defclass-internal`
2. Add metadata directly to slot definitions
3. Auto-generate command-line args from slot metadata
4. Auto-generate transient infixes from slot metadata  
5. Auto-generate parse functions from slot metadata

## Slot Property Schema

### CLI Properties
| Property | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `:long-option` | Long CLI option | `"--title"` |
| `:short-option` | Short CLI option | `"-t"` |
| `:option-type` | Serialization type | `:string`, `:boolean`, `:list` |
| `:positional` | Position index (1, 2, 3...) or nil | `1` |
| `:option-separator` | List separator | `","` |

### Transient Properties
| Property | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `:transient-key` | Key binding | `"t"` |
| `:transient-description` | Description | `"Title (required)"` |
| `:transient-class` | Transient class | `transient-option` |
| `:transient-reader` | Reader function | `beads-reader-issue-title` |
| `:transient-choices` | Valid choices | `'("bug" "feature")` |
| `:transient-prompt` | Input prompt | `"Title: "` |
| `:transient-level` | Visibility level | `1` |
| `:transient-group` | Group name | `"Required"` |
| `:transient-order` | Order in group | `1` |

### Validation Properties
| Property | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `:required` | Required field? | `t` |
| `:validator` | Validation function | `beads-validate-title` |

## Benefits

- Reduce ~1500 lines of boilerplate code
- Single source of truth for slot metadata
- Automatic consistency between CLI and transient
- Easier to add new commands
- Better maintainability

## Reference

Based on closql approach: https://github.com/magit/closql
title: EIEIO Meta-Programming: Reduce boilerplate with slot metadata

description:
Create a comprehensive ERT test that implements the bd quickstart guide using the test DSL. This serves as both validation of the DSL and executable documentation.

## Important Constraints
- **MUST run in a temporary directory** - never in current working directory
- Test must be fully isolated and leave no artifacts
- **Requires bd CLI** - uses `skip-unless (executable-find beads-executable)`
- **This IS a test** - will be in lisp/test/ and run via `eldev test`

## IMPORTANT: Hash-Based IDs
bd 0.21.5+ uses **hash-based IDs** (e.g., `myproject-a3f8`) not sequential IDs.
All assertions must use dynamic ID lookup, NOT hardcoded IDs:

```elisp
;; WRONG - assumes sequential IDs
(beads-test-assert-issue-has ctx "myproject-1" :status "closed")

;; CORRECT - use dynamic lookup
(beads-test-assert-issue-has ctx (car (oref ctx created-issues)) :status "closed")
```

## Test File
```
lisp/test/beads-quickstart-test.el
```

## Quickstart Steps to Implement

Based on the bd quickstart guide, implement these as DSL actions:

### 1. Initialize Project
```bash
bd init --prefix myproject
```
- Create temp directory
- Run bd init with custom prefix
- Verify .beads directory created

### 2. Create First Issue
```bash
bd create "Set up CI/CD pipeline" -t task -p 1
```
- Create issue with title, type, priority
- Verify issue created with correct fields
- **Store returned ID for later assertions**

### 3. Create Related Issues
```bash
bd create "Write unit tests" -t task -p 2
bd create "Fix login bug" -t bug -p 0
```
- Create multiple issues
- Verify different types and priorities

### 4. List Issues
```bash
bd list
bd list --status open
bd ready
```
- Open list buffer
- Verify issues appear
- Test filtering

### 5. Show Issue Details
```bash
bd show <first-issue-id>
```
- Navigate to issue in list
- Open show buffer
- Verify content displayed
- **Use dynamic ID from step 2**

### 6. Update Issue Status
```bash
bd update <first-issue-id> --status in_progress
```
- Update status
- Verify change persisted

### 7. Add Dependencies
```bash
bd dep add <second-issue-id> <first-issue-id> --type blocks
```
- Add dependency between issues
- Verify with bd blocked

### 8. Close Issue
```bash
bd close <first-issue-id> --reason "Completed"
```
- Close issue with reason
- Verify status is closed

### 9. View Statistics
```bash
bd stats
```
- View project statistics
- Verify counts match expectations

## Implementation Pattern

```elisp
(beads-define-workflow-test beads-quickstart-full-guide
  :description "Execute the complete bd quickstart guide as an integration test"
  :tags (:integration :quickstart)
  :steps
  ((beads-test-action-init :prefix "myproject")
   (beads-test-action-create-issue 
    :title "Set up CI/CD pipeline" 
    :type "task" 
    :priority 1)
   ;; ... more steps
   )
  :assert
  ((beads-test-assert-issue-count ctx 3)
   ;; Use dynamic IDs from context, not hardcoded values
   (let ((first-id (nth 0 (reverse (oref ctx created-issues)))))
     (beads-test-assert-issue-has ctx first-id :status "closed"))))
```

### New Actions Needed
- `beads-test-action-init` - Initialize bd in temp directory with prefix
- `beads-test-action-add-dependency` - Add dependency between issues
- `beads-test-action-show-stats` - View statistics

## Running the Test
```bash
# Run just this test
BD_NO_DAEMON=1 guix shell -D -f guix.scm -- eldev -p -dtT test beads-quickstart-full-guide

# Run all quickstart tests
BD_NO_DAEMON=1 guix shell -D -f guix.scm -- eldev -p -dtT test --tags quickstart
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Test file created: lisp/test/beads-quickstart-test.el
- [ ] Test runs completely in isolated temp directory
- [ ] All 9 quickstart steps implemented as actions
- [ ] **All assertions use dynamic ID lookup (no hardcoded IDs)**
- [ ] Test passes end-to-end with real bd commands
- [ ] No artifacts left after test completion
- [ ] Can run test multiple times without conflicts
- [ ] Test discoverable via `eldev test`
- [ ] Test tagged with :integration and :quickstart
- [ ] Serves as executable documentation of bd workflow
title: Implement bd quickstart guide as executable ERT test

description:
Add `beads-worktree-session` EIEIO class to beads-sesman.el.

## Key Change
**Session identity is project root directory, not branch.**

## Purpose
Represent a project/worktree context that groups all beads buffers and agent sessions. The session is keyed by directory path, which is stable and unique.

## Implementation
```elisp
(defclass beads-worktree-session ()
  ((id
    :initarg :id
    :type string
    :documentation "Unique ID: abbreviated project path (e.g., ~/code/beads.el).")
   (project-dir
    :initarg :project-dir
    :type string
    :documentation "Project root directory (absolute). THIS IS THE IDENTITY.")
   (project-name
    :initarg :project-name
    :type string
    :documentation "Project name (basename of project-dir).")
   (branch
    :initarg :branch
    :initform nil
    :type (or string null)
    :documentation "Current git branch. METADATA, can change, refreshed on access.")
   (buffers
    :initarg :buffers
    :initform nil
    :type list
    :documentation "List of beads buffers (list, show) in this session.")
   (agent-sessions
    :initarg :agent-sessions
    :initform nil
    :type list
    :documentation "List of agent sessions in this project."))
  "Represents a beads project/worktree context for sesman.")
```

## Session ID Format
Session ID is the abbreviated project path:
- `~/code/beads.el` (abbreviated home)
- `beads.el` (just project name if unique)
- NOT `project@branch` (branch is not identity)

## Acceptance Criteria

### Class Definition
- [ ] Class defined with all 6 slots
- [ ] project-dir is the IDENTITY (emphasized in docs)
- [ ] branch is METADATA (clearly documented as changeable)
- [ ] Proper :type declarations

### Session ID
- [ ] Uses abbreviated path, not branch
- [ ] Different directories → different IDs
- [ ] Same directory, different branch → SAME ID

### Slot Behavior
- [ ] `id` - abbreviated path, immutable after creation
- [ ] `project-dir` - absolute path, THE IDENTITY
- [ ] `project-name` - basename for display
- [ ] `branch` - can be nil, can change, metadata only
- [ ] `buffers` - list of live buffer objects
- [ ] `agent-sessions` - list of agent session objects

### Helper Methods
- [ ] `beads-worktree-session-empty-p` - t if no buffers and no agents
- [ ] `beads-worktree-session-refresh-branch` - update branch metadata

### Testing
- [ ] Unit test: class instantiation
- [ ] Unit test: session ID is path-based
- [ ] Unit test: same dir different branch → same session

### Quality
- [ ] No byte-compilation warnings
- [ ] Passes lint checks
- [ ] EIEIO properly required
title: Add beads-worktree-session EIEIO class (directory-keyed)

description:
Add intelligent auto-completion for labels in all reader functions that prompt for labels.

Currently, users must manually type label names when filtering by labels or adding labels to issues. This epic will add auto-completion that fetches existing labels from the database using 'bd label list-all' and provides them as completion candidates.

## Scope

Update all label reader functions to use completion with cached label data:
- beads-reader-list-label (for --label filter in bd list)
- beads-reader-list-labels (for multiple labels)
- Any other readers that prompt for label input

## Implementation Details

1. Add a label fetching function that calls 'bd label list-all --json'
2. Parse the JSON output into beads-label objects (defined in beads-types.el)
3. Extract unique label names from the results
4. Implement caching with TTL (similar to beads--completion-cache pattern)
5. Update reader functions to use completing-read with label list
6. Allow multiple label selection where appropriate (comma-separated or repeated calls)

## Benefits

- Faster label entry with auto-completion
- Consistency in label naming (uses existing labels)
- Discovery of available labels without leaving Emacs
- Reduced typos in label names
- Better UX for filtering and tagging issues
notes:
## Implementation Complete

Successfully implemented label auto-completion for all label readers:

### Changes Made

1. **beads.el**: Added label fetching and caching functions
   - beads-label-list-all: Fetches labels from 'bd label list-all --json'
   - beads--get-cached-labels: Gets cached labels with TTL (300 seconds)
   - beads--invalidate-label-cache: Invalidates cache after label changes
   - beads--label-completion-table: Returns completion table of label names
   - beads-label-cache-ttl: Configurable TTL (default 5 minutes)

2. **beads-reader.el**: Updated three reader functions
   - beads-reader-list-label: Single label with completion (AND filter)
   - beads-reader-list-labels: Multiple labels with completing-read-multiple
   - beads-reader-issue-labels: Issue labels with completing-read-multiple
   - All fall back to read-string when no labels exist

3. **Tests**: Comprehensive test coverage added

description:
Add an Emacs version of 'bd epic status' that provides an interactive buffer for viewing and navigating epics with their sub-issues.

## Current State

The bd CLI command 'bd epic status' shows all epics with progress:
```
○ beads.el-7bea Update beads.el for beads 0.21.5 compatibility
   Progress: 3/17 children closed (17%)

○ beads.el-1 beads.el: Magit-like Emacs interface for Beads
   Progress: 3/11 children closed (27%)
```

## Desired State

An interactive Emacs buffer with:
1. **TAB to expand/collapse**: Toggle display of sub-issues inline
2. **RET to navigate**: Open sub-issues in beads-list view
3. **Dynamic fetching**: Fetch sub-issues only when expanded
4. **Visual hierarchy**: Show sub-issues indented with metadata
5. **Refresh support**: g key to refresh epic status

## Example Output (expanded)

```
○ beads.el-7bea Update beads.el for beads 0.21.5 compatibility
   Progress: 3/17 children closed (17%)

○ beads.el-1 beads.el: Magit-like Emacs interface for Beads
   Progress: 3/11 children closed (27%)
     beads.el-n9h [P3] [task] closed - Add --json global flag
     beads.el-b63 [P3] [task] closed - Add --db global flag
     beads.el-2fz [P3] [task] closed - Add --actor global flag

○ beads.el-57 Epic: Integration tests for WORKFLOW.md scenarios
   Progress: 0/6 children closed (0%)
```

## User Benefits

- Quick overview of all epics and their progress
- Fast navigation to epic sub-issues
- Expand/collapse for focused viewing
- No need to run separate bd commands to see children
- Integrated with beads.el workflow
title: Add beads-epic-status command with interactive sub-issue display

description:
When starting an agent, beads-agent--fetch-issue-async uses start-process which allocates a pty. This causes bd to emit terminal escape sequences that corrupt the JSON output.

**Root cause:**
start-process defaults to pty connection, triggering bd's terminal detection.

**Solution:**
Use make-process with :connection-type 'pipe instead of start-process in beads-agent--fetch-issue-async and other async functions.

**Test to reproduce:**
```elisp
;; This fails with escape sequences:
(let ((buf (generate-new-buffer "*test*")))
  (start-process "test" buf beads-executable "show" "--json" "test-8hb")
  (sleep-for 2)
  (with-current-buffer buf (buffer-string)))
;; => "^[]11;?^[\\^[[6n"

;; This works:
(let ((buf (generate-new-buffer "*test*")))
  (make-process :name "test" :buffer buf
                :command (list beads-executable "show" "--json" "test-8hb")
                :connection-type 'pipe)
  (sleep-for 2)
  (with-current-buffer buf (buffer-string)))
;; => proper JSON
```
title: beads-agent async fetch fails due to start-process pty allocation

description:

Location: beads-create.el (and possibly other modules)

Current pattern:


Should be:


Solution:
1. Identify all uses of append in iteration across codebase
2. Replace with push/nreverse pattern
3. Verify argument order is preserved (nreverse important!)
4. Run tests to ensure behavior unchanged
5. Document pattern in code comments if not obvious

Performance impact: Minimal for small arg lists, but establishes good practice.
title: Refactor append iteration to push/nreverse for O(n) performance

close_reason: Implemented beads-list-follow-mode with magit-like behavior for following issues. Changed buffer naming to project-based, added debounced update mechanism with 0.2s idle timer.
description:
Implement magit-log style navigation for beads-show buffer.

**Problem:**
When browsing issues in beads-list, users must manually navigate to each issue
to see details. This is inefficient compared to magit-log where moving through
commits automatically updates a companion diff/revision buffer.

**Solution:**
Implement `beads-list-follow-mode`, a minor mode that automatically updates
a visible beads-show buffer when navigating issues in beads-list buffers.

**Magit Behavior Analysis:**
Magit uses these patterns for follow-mode:
1. `magit-section-movement-hook` - Called after navigation commands
2. `run-with-idle-timer` (0.2s default) - Debounces rapid navigation
3. Buffer detection - Only updates if show buffer exists in frame
4. Non-selecting display - Updates without stealing focus

**User Experience:**
- Toggle with `F` key in beads-list buffers
- When enabled and a beads-show buffer is visible: n/p navigation
  automatically updates the show buffer with the current issue's details
- Configurable delay to prevent updates during rapid navigation
- Non-intrusive: show buffer updates without focus change
notes:
Plan created. Key changes:
1. New minor mode `beads-list-follow-mode` with F keybinding
2. Idle timer debouncing pattern (matching magit's approach)
3. New `beads-show-noselect` for non-selecting display
4. ~205 lines of new code across 4 files (80 list, 25 show, 100 tests)
title: Implement magit-log like behavior for following issues in beads-show

description:
Build an Emacs Lisp package that provides a Magit-like porcelain interface for Beads issue tracking. The package should live in the lisp/ directory and provide an intuitive, keyboard-driven UI for working with Beads issues directly from Emacs.

Key features:
- Status buffer showing issues by status (open, in_progress, blocked, closed)
- Keyboard shortcuts for common operations (create, update, close, show details)
- Integration with existing beads CLI via subprocess calls
- Transient menus for complex operations (similar to Magit's interface)
- Jump to issue definitions in code
- Inline editing of issue fields
- Dependency visualization
- Integration with Emacs project.el for multi-project support

This will make Beads more accessible to Emacs users and provide a seamless workflow for AI-supervised coding within the editor.
notes:
**Core Goal Update (2025-10-18)**: The primary objective of beads.el is to provide complete 1-to-1 coverage of the Beads CLI. For every 'bd' command and flag, there should be a corresponding Emacs command accessible through the beads.el interface. Users should be able to perform all bd operations without leaving Emacs.

This means:
- Every bd subcommand (init, create, list, show, update, close, ready, blocked, stats, dep, export, import) must have an Emacs equivalent
- All flags and options for each command must be supported in the transient menus
- The Emacs interface should provide the same level of control as the CLI, just with better UX for Emacs users

**New BD CLI Commands Requiring Emacs Implementation (Updated 2025-10-18)**:
The following bd commands exist in the CLI but do not have Emacs implementations yet:

1. **compact** - Compact old closed issues using semantic summarization (tier 1: 30 days, tier 2: 90 days)
2. **daemon** - Run background sync daemon with auto-commit/push capabilities (may be automatic in Emacs context)
3. **delete** - Delete an issue and clean up all references (destructive operation)
4. **epic** - Epic management commands (NEW: subcommands for working with epics)
5. **label** - Manage issue labels (subcommands: add, list, list-all, remove)
6. **rename-prefix** - Rename the issue prefix for all issues in the database
7. **renumber** - Renumber all issues sequentially to eliminate ID gaps
8. **reopen** - Reopen closed issues (more explicit than update --status open)
9. **restore** - Restore full history of a compacted issue from git
10. **sync** - Synchronize issues with git remote in single operation

**Already Implemented Commands**:
The following bd commands already have Emacs implementations:
- blocked, close, create, dep (add/remove/tree/cycles), export, import, init, list, quickstart, ready, show, stats, update

**Commands Not Requiring Emacs Implementation**:
- completion (Emacs has its own completion system)
- help (covered by Emacs help system)
- version (not essential; could be shown in beads main menu if needed)

**Note on Global Flags**:
The bd CLI now has a --no-daemon flag that should be supported in all transient menus alongside existing global flags (--actor, --db, --json, --no-auto-flush, --no-auto-import).
title: beads.el: Magit-like Emacs interface for Beads

description:
Update agent indicators in beads-list for directory-bound agent model.

## Key Change
Show project-level agent activity, not just issue-specific.

## File to Modify
`lisp/beads-list.el`

## Current Indicator (Issue-Bound)
Shows if an agent is working ON this specific issue:
- `T#1` - Task agent #1 on this issue

## New Indicator (Directory-Bound)
Shows if any project agent has touched/focused on this issue:
- `[P]` - A project agent has touched this issue
- `[P:T]` - A project agent is focused on this issue (type T)
- `[P:T#1]` - Specific agent instance focused on this issue

## Query Function Changes
```elisp
;; Old: get sessions for this issue
(beads-agent--get-sessions-for-issue issue-id)

;; New: get sessions that touched this issue
(beads-agent--get-sessions-touching-issue issue-id)

;; New: get sessions focused on this issue
(beads-agent--get-sessions-focused-on-issue issue-id)
```

## Display Logic
```elisp
(defun beads-list--format-agent (issue-id)
  "Format agent indicator for ISSUE-ID."
  (let ((focused (beads-agent--get-sessions-focused-on-issue issue-id))
        (touched (beads-agent--get-sessions-touching-issue issue-id)))
    (cond
     (focused (format "[P:%s]" (format-focused-agents focused)))
     (touched "[P]")
     (t ""))))
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Shows if issue was touched by any agent
- [ ] Shows if issue is current focus
- [ ] Shows agent type when focused
- [ ] Query functions updated for directory model
- [ ] Help-echo shows session details
- [ ] Tests updated
title: Update agent indicators in beads-list for directory-bound model

description:
Remove CLI-based plan mode infrastructure (`requires-plan-mode`, `--permission-mode plan`) and implement plan mode purely through prompts. This unifies plan mode behavior across all backends.

## Problem

Currently the Plan agent type:
- Sets `requires-plan-mode: t` on the type
- Returns `nil` from `build-prompt` (expecting CLI to handle it)
- Requires backends to implement `supports-plan-mode-p`
- Only works with backends that support `--permission-mode plan` flag

This creates inconsistency - Plan agent works on claude-code-ide but not on agent-shell.

## Solution

Make Plan agent work like Task/Review/QA - use a prompt that instructs the agent to plan without making changes. All backends can then support Plan agent uniformly.

## Changes Required

### Remove from beads-agent-type.el
- `requires-plan-mode` slot from `beads-agent-type` class (lines 66-72)
- Simplify `beads-agent-type-validate-backend` to just return t (lines 115-129)

### Remove from beads-agent-backend.el
- `beads-agent-backend-supports-plan-mode-p` generic function (lines 216-228)

### Remove from beads-agent-claude-code-ide.el
- `beads-agent-backend-supports-plan-mode-p` method (lines 189-194)

### Update beads-agent-types.el
- Add `beads-agent-type-plan--prompt` constant with planning instructions
- Update `beads-agent-type-plan` class to remove `requires-plan-mode` slot
- Implement `beads-agent-type-build-prompt` for Plan type (currently returns nil)

### Update beads-agent.el
- Remove validation call at lines 644-645
- Update docstring for `beads-agent-start-plan` (line 1388-1394)

### Update tests
- Remove plan mode validation tests in beads-agent-type-test.el (lines 425-473)
- Update Plan type tests in beads-agent-types-test.el (lines 178-193)
- Remove supports-plan-mode tests in beads-agent-test.el (lines 1542-1599)

## Plan Agent Prompt

The Plan agent prompt should instruct:
1. DO NOT modify any files - only read and analyze
2. DO NOT execute commands that change state
3. Create detailed, actionable implementation plans
4. Identify files to modify, potential risks, dependencies
5. Output structured plan for human review

## Benefits

1. **Uniform behavior**: Plan mode works identically on all backends
2. **Simpler code**: No backend capability checking
3. **Extensible**: Easy to customize planning prompt via defcustom later
4. **No CLI dependencies**: Works with any backend that accepts prompts
title: Simplify Plan agent to use prompt-based mode instead of CLI flags

description: Document the integration testing patterns, best practices, and templates developed during this epic so other developers can easily add tests for new transient menus.
notes:
Document beads-test-interact pattern:

The beads-test-interact function (lisp/test/beads-test.el:74) is a key utility for testing transient menus. It executes keyboard macro commands while preserving default-directory, avoiding issues with execute-kbd-macro.

Usage pattern:
  (beads-test-interact '("t Title RET" "x"))

This simulates typing 't', then 'Title', then RET, then 'x' - perfect for testing transient menu interactions.

Key characteristics:
- Preserves default-directory (unlike execute-kbd-macro)
- Executes pre-command-hook and post-command-hook for realistic simulation
- Handles undo-boundary correctly
- Works with call-interactively to trigger proper transient hooks

Examples in codebase:
- beads-list-test.el:1002 - Quit transient with 'q'
- beads-list-test.el:1023 - Execute list command with 'x'
- beads-create-test.el:275 - Set title and execute
- beads-create-test.el:309-314 - Full workflow with all fields

See StackOverflow reference in beads-list-test.el:983 for original inspiration.

---

Document beads-test-with-project pattern:

The beads-test-with-project macro (lisp/test/beads-test.el:41) creates a temporary beads project for integration testing. Essential for tests that need a real bd database.

Usage patterns:
  (beads-test-with-project ()
    ;; test code with default project settings
    )

  (beads-test-with-project (:prefix "myproject" :quiet t)
    ;; test code with custom init args
    )

What it does:
- Creates temporary directory with make-temp-file
- Initializes beads project via beads-command-init
- Sets default-directory to the temp project
- Clears beads--project-cache to avoid cross-test pollution
- Mocks beads--find-project-root to return nil (forces use of temp dir instead of discovering main repo)

Key benefits:
- Provides isolated test environment with real bd database
- No need to mock bd command execution
- Tests actual bd CLI behavior and JSON output
- Automatic cleanup via temp directory
- Prevents tests from interfering with each other

Usage in integration tests:
- beads-delete-test.el:272 - Full delete workflow
- beads-export-test.el:81 - Export command testing
- beads-create-test.el:300 - Full creation workflow with all fields
- Used in almost every :integration tagged test

Common pattern:
  (skip-unless (executable-find beads-executable))
  (beads-test-with-project ()
    ;; Create test data
    (let ((issue (beads-command-create! :title "Test")))
      ;; Test operations on the data
      ...))
title: Create TESTING.md documentation for integration testing patterns

description:
Implement Emacs interface for the 'bd compact' command.

The bd compact command uses semantic summarization to reduce database size by compacting old closed issues. This is permanent graceful decay where original content is discarded.

**Features:**
- Two compression tiers:
  - Tier 1: Semantic compression (30 days closed, 70% reduction)
  - Tier 2: Ultra compression (90 days closed, 95% reduction)
- Parallel processing with configurable workers
- Batch processing with configurable batch size
- Preview mode with --dry-run
- Statistics display
- Force compact specific issues

**Implementation:**
- Create beads-compact.el module
- Transient menu with all flags:
  - --all: Process all candidates
  - --dry-run: Preview without compacting
  - --stats: Show compaction statistics
  - --id: Compact specific issue
  - --force: Force compact (bypass checks, requires --id)
  - --tier: Compaction tier (1 or 2)
  - --batch-size: Issues per batch
  - --workers: Parallel workers
- Display results in dedicated buffer
- Show before/after statistics
- Confirmation prompt for destructive operations
title: beads.el: Implement bd compact command

description:
Create the beads-meta.el module that provides infrastructure for custom EIEIO slot properties.

## Implementation

1. Define `beads-meta--slot-properties` constant listing all custom properties
2. Add `define-advice` on `eieio-defclass-internal` to preserve custom properties
3. Add `define-advice` on `eieio--slot-override` to handle inheritance
4. Implement helper functions:
   - `beads-meta-slot-property` - Get a property for a slot
   - `beads-meta-slot-properties` - Get all properties for a slot
   - `beads-meta-slots-with-property` - Find slots that have a property
   - `beads-meta-command-slots` - Get all slots for a command class

## Custom Properties to Support

### CLI Properties
- `:long-option` - Long CLI option name (e.g., "--title")
- `:short-option` - Short CLI option name (e.g., "-t")
- `:option-type` - Type for serialization (:string, :boolean, :integer, :list)
- `:positional` - Integer position for positional args (1, 2, 3...), nil for named options
- `:option-separator` - Separator for :list type (default ",")

### Transient Properties
- `:transient-key` - Key binding in transient menu
- `:transient-description` - Description in transient
- `:transient-class` - Transient class (transient-option, transient-switch, etc.)
- `:transient-reader` - Reader function for input
- `:transient-choices` - Valid choices list
- `:transient-prompt` - Input prompt string
- `:transient-level` - Menu visibility level (1-7)
- `:transient-group` - Group name for organization
- `:transient-order` - Order within group (lower = first)

### Validation Properties
- `:required` - Is field required?
- `:validator` - Validation function

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] beads-meta.el created with proper header/commentary
- [ ] Custom slot properties preserved after defclass
- [ ] Slot property inheritance works correctly
- [ ] Helper functions implemented and documented
- [ ] ERT tests in test/beads-meta-test.el
- [ ] Lint and compile pass
title: Create beads-meta.el module with slot property infrastructure

description:
Add intelligent auto-completion for labels in all reader functions that prompt for labels.

Currently, users must manually type label names when filtering by labels or adding labels to issues. This epic will add auto-completion that fetches existing labels from the database using 'bd label list-all' and provides them as completion candidates.

## Scope

Update all label reader functions to use completion with cached label data:
- beads-reader-list-label (for --label filter in bd list)
- beads-reader-list-labels (for multiple labels)
- Any other readers that prompt for label input

## Implementation Details

1. Add a label fetching function that calls 'bd label list-all --json'
2. Parse the JSON output into beads-label objects (defined in beads-types.el)
3. Extract unique label names from the results
4. Implement caching with TTL (similar to beads--completion-cache pattern)
5. Update reader functions to use completing-read with label list
6. Allow multiple label selection where appropriate (comma-separated or repeated calls)

## Benefits

- Faster label entry with auto-completion
- Consistency in label naming (uses existing labels)
- Discovery of available labels without leaving Emacs
- Reduced typos in label names
- Better UX for filtering and tagging issues
notes:
## Implementation Complete

Successfully implemented label auto-completion for all label readers:

### Changes Made

1. **beads.el**: Added label fetching and caching functions
   - beads-label-list-all: Fetches labels from 'bd label list-all --json'
   - beads--get-cached-labels: Gets cached labels with TTL (300 seconds)
   - beads--invalidate-label-cache: Invalidates cache after label changes
   - beads--label-completion-table: Returns completion table of label names
   - beads-label-cache-ttl: Configurable TTL (default 5 minutes)

2. **beads-reader.el**: Updated three reader functions
   - beads-reader-list-label: Single label with completion (AND filter)
   - beads-reader-list-labels: Multiple labels with completing-read-multiple
   - beads-reader-issue-labels: Issue labels with completing-read-multiple
   - All fall back to read-string when no labels exist

3. **Tests**: Comprehensive test coverage added
   - lisp/test/beads-test.el: 9 new tests for label fetching and caching
   - lisp/test/beads-reader-test.el: 7 new tests for reader completion
   - Tests cover: success cases, empty labels, caching, expiry, invalidation

### Quality Checks

- **Tests**: 1078 tests, 1076 passed (99.8%)
  - 1 pre-existing integration test failure (unrelated)
  - All new label tests pass
- **Lint**: All files pass with zero warnings
- **Compile**: Clean compilation with zero new warnings

### Benefits

✓ Faster label entry with auto-completion
✓ Consistency in label naming (uses existing labels)
✓ Discovery of available labels without leaving Emacs
✓ Reduced typos in label names
✓ Better UX for filtering and tagging issues
title: Add label auto-completion to all label readers

description:
In beads-show buffer, sub-issues are rendered with leading padding:

```
Sub-issues  (0/8 completed)
──────────

  guix.vc-0drh [P1] [task] OPEN - Create chat.scm...
  guix.vc-0o7p [P1] [task] OPEN - Implement chat...
```

When pressing TAB to navigate between issue links, point lands at column 0 (the beginning of the line, on the padding spaces) instead of on the first letter of the issue ID.

Expected behavior: TAB should position point on the first character of the issue ID (e.g., the 'g' in 'guix.vc-0drh'), not on the leading whitespace.
title: Navigate to first letter of issue ID when TABbing to sub-issues in beads-show

description:
## Location
`lisp/beads-list.el:708-710` - `beads-list-refresh-all`

## Problem
Catches and ignores ALL errors from individual buffer refreshes:
```elisp
(condition-case nil
    (beads-list-refresh 'silent)
  (error nil))  ;; Silent failure!
```

If there's a pattern of failures, users won't know why list isn't updating.

## Fix
Log errors in debug mode.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Errors logged when debugging
- [ ] Silent in normal use
- [ ] Test coverage
- [ ] Tested in live Emacs session inside tmux
title: Log errors in beads-list-refresh-all

description:
Implement Emacs interface for the bd rename-prefix command.

The rename-prefix command renames the issue prefix for all issues in
the database (e.g., from "bd" to "project").

Features:
- Transient menu for rename-prefix
- Show current prefix
- Input field for new prefix with validation
- Strong confirmation prompt showing:
  - Current prefix
  - New prefix
  - Number of issues to be renamed
- Display results (number of issues renamed)
- Invalidate all caches after rename
- Refresh all open buffers

This is a major operation that affects all issues and should have
strong confirmation with clear warnings about the impact.
title: beads.el: Implement bd rename-prefix command

notes:
## Summary

Extend beads.el's existing EIEIO command infrastructure to cover ALL bd CLI commands with auto-generated transient menus, providing a complete Magit-like interface.

## Technical Context

- **Language/Version**: Emacs Lisp (Emacs 27.1+)
- **Primary Dependencies**: transient, EIEIO (built-in), vterm/eat/term (optional)
- **Testing**: ERT with mocking, integration tests using temporary beads repos
- **Target Platform**: GNU Emacs 27.1+, GUI and terminal modes
- **Scale/Scope**: ~50 bd commands to implement, organized into ~15 elisp files
- **Performance Goals**: Transient menus appear instantly (<100ms)

## Design Artifacts

- **Spec**: specs/001-eieio-command-system/spec.md
- **Plan**: specs/001-eieio-command-system/plan.md
- **Data Model**: specs/001-eieio-command-system/data-model.md
- **Contracts**: specs/001-eieio-command-system/contracts/generic-methods.md
- **Quickstart**: specs/001-eieio-command-system/quickstart.md
- **Tasks**: specs/001-eieio-command-system/tasks.md

## Functional Requirements

- FR-001: Main transient menu (`M-x beads`) matching `bd --help` structure
- FR-002: One elisp file per top-level command
- FR-003: EIEIO classes for ALL beads commands
- FR-004: Developers populate EIEIO meta slots from `bd <command> --help`
- FR-005: Auto-generate interactive commands from class definitions
- FR-006: Auto-generated commands prompt for positional arguments
- FR-007: Transient menus allow modifying all arguments and options
- FR-008: Execute, Preview, Reset actions in all transients
- FR-009: Customizable actions per class via generic methods
- FR-010: Default execute/render behavior without specializers
- FR-011: Terminal fallback chain: vterm → eat → term → compilation-mode
- FR-012: Classes can override execution/rendering via generic methods
- FR-013: Integration tests for each command with temp repos
- FR-014: Integration tests must not pollute working directory
- FR-015: Org-based manual compilable to Info mode
- FR-016: Manual contains transient menu previews
- FR-017: Previews stay synchronized with actual transients
- FR-018: Commands testable in non-graphical Emacs (tmux)

## Success Criteria

- SC-001: All `bd --help` commands have EIEIO classes and interactive commands
- SC-002: Any command reachable in ≤3 interactions from `M-x beads`
- SC-003: 100% of commands work with default behavior
- SC-004: 85%+ code coverage for command execution paths
- SC-005: Zero working directory pollution from tests
- SC-006: Info manual has accurate transient previews
- SC-007: All commands work in GUI and terminal Emacs
- SC-008: Preview shows exact command that will execute

## Acceptance Criteria (Quality Gates)

- **Test Coverage**: >85% code coverage for all new command modules
- **Test Speed**: Full test suite completes in <60 seconds
- **Per-Module Tests**: Each command module tests run in <5 seconds
- **Code Quality**: Zero byte-compilation warnings, zero lint warnings
- **CI Green**: All GitHub Actions workflows pass

## Key Decisions

- Use existing beads-meta slot property system
- Follow beads-command-doctor pattern as reference
- Terminal backend fallback chain for rendering
- Use `result` slot (not `data`) for command output

## User Stories

1. **US1** [P1]: Access Main Command Menu
2. **US2** [P1]: Execute Command via Transient
3. **US3** [P1]: Auto-Generated Interactive Commands
4. **US4** [P2]: Customize Command Rendering
5. **US5** [P2]: View Documentation with Live Previews
6. **US6** [P2]: Run Integration Tests
7. **US7** [P3]: Test Commands in Non-Graphical Emacs

## Child Epics

- bde-koh7.1: Setup & Foundation (blocking)
- bde-koh7.2: US1 - Access Main Command Menu
- bde-koh7.3: US2 - Execute Command via Transient
- bde-koh7.4: US3 - Auto-Generated Interactive Commands
- bde-koh7.5: US4 - Customize Command Rendering
- bde-koh7.6: US5 - View Documentation with Live Previews
- bde-koh7.7: US6 - Run Integration Tests
- bde-koh7.8: US7 - Test Commands in Non-Graphical Emacs
- bde-koh7.9: Additional Command Coverage
- bde-koh7.10: Polish & Cross-Cutting Concerns
title: EIEIO Command System for Beads.el

## User Story

A user wants to quickly access all Beads commands from Emacs without remembering individual command names. They invoke `M-x beads` and see a transient menu mirroring `bd --help` structure, allowing navigation to any command or command group.

## Why P1 Priority

This is the primary entry point for the entire Beads.el experience. Without this, users cannot discover or access any functionality.

## Independent Test

Can be fully tested by invoking `M-x beads` and verifying the transient menu appears with correct command groups and subcommands listed.

## Acceptance Scenarios

1. **Given** Emacs is running with beads.el loaded, **When** user invokes `M-x beads`, **Then** a transient menu appears showing all top-level commands and command groups matching `bd --help` output
2. **Given** the main transient menu is displayed, **When** user selects a command group (e.g., "daemon"), **Then** a sub-menu appears with that group's commands
3. **Given** the main transient menu is displayed, **When** user selects a direct command (e.g., "create"), **Then** that command's transient menu opens

## Tasks

- T010: Audit `bd --help` output to list all top-level commands and groups
- T011: Update `lisp/beads-main.el` to include all command groups
- T012: [P] Add autoload for `beads-ready` in Views group
- T013: [P] Add autoload for `beads-blocked` in Views group
- T014: [P] Add autoload for `beads-stale` in Views group
- T015: [P] Add autoload for `beads-daemon` group
- T016: [P] Add autoload for `beads-config`
- T017: [P] Add autoload for `beads-hooks`
- T018: Verify navigation from main menu to command groups opens sub-menus
- T019: Verify navigation from main menu to direct commands opens command transient

## Checkpoint

User Story 1 complete - `M-x beads` shows full command menu

## Success Criteria

- SC-002: Any command reachable in ≤3 interactions from `M-x beads`

notes:
## User Story

A user wants to create a new issue using `bd create`. They invoke the command, fill in positional arguments and options via the transient interface, preview the command, and execute it.

## Why P1 Priority

Command execution is the core functionality. Users must be able to run commands with full control over arguments and options.

## Independent Test

Can be fully tested by invoking `M-x beads-create`, filling in required fields, previewing, and executing the command against a test beads repository.

## Acceptance Scenarios

1. **Given** user invokes `M-x beads-create`, **When** the transient menu appears, **Then** it shows fields for all positional arguments and command-specific options
2. **Given** the create transient is open with fields filled, **When** user selects "Preview", **Then** the exact `bd create` command that would be executed is displayed
3. **Given** the create transient is open with valid inputs, **When** user selects "Execute", **Then** the command runs and output is displayed appropriately
4. **Given** the create transient is open, **When** user selects "Reset", **Then** all fields return to default/empty state

## Tasks

- T020: Verify existing `beads-create.el` implements full workflow (preview, execute, reset)
- T021: Verify `beads-command-preview` shows exact command line
- T022: [P] Implement `beads-command-ready` class (reference: quickstart.md)
- T023: [P] Create `beads-ready.el` transient using `beads-meta-define-transient`
- T024: [P] Implement `beads-command-blocked` class
- T025: [P] Create `beads-blocked.el` transient
- T026: [P] Implement `beads-command-stale` class
- T027: [P] Create `beads-stale.el` transient
- T028: [P] Implement `beads-command-count` class
- T029: [P] Create `beads-count.el` transient
- T030: Verify Reset action clears all fields

## Checkpoint

User Story 2 complete - full transient workflow works for all view commands

## Success Criteria

- SC-008: Preview shows exact command that will execute

notes:
## User Story

A developer defining a new EIEIO command class wants an interactive Emacs command auto-generated. They define `beads-command-daemon-list` class with metadata slots, and `M-x beads-daemon-list` becomes available automatically.

## Why P1 Priority

Auto-generation eliminates boilerplate and ensures consistency. Every command class automatically gets a usable interface.

## Independent Test

Can be tested by defining a minimal command class and verifying the corresponding `M-x` command exists and opens a transient.

## Acceptance Scenarios

1. **Given** a class `beads-command-foo` is defined with required metadata, **When** beads.el loads, **Then** `M-x beads-foo` command is available
2. **Given** `M-x beads-daemon-list` is invoked, **When** the command requires positional arguments, **Then** user is prompted for them before transient opens
3. **Given** the transient opens for a command, **When** positional arguments were provided, **Then** they appear in the transient and can be modified

## Tasks

- T031: Verify `beads-meta-define-transient` generates `;;;###autoload` cookie
- T032: [P] Implement `beads-command-daemon` abstract base class
- T033: [P] Implement `beads-command-daemon-list` class
- T034: [P] Implement `beads-command-daemon-start` class
- T035: [P] Implement `beads-command-daemon-stop` class
- T036: [P] Implement `beads-command-daemon-status` class
- T037: Create `beads-daemon.el` with transients for all daemon commands
- T038: Verify positional argument prompting works before transient opens
- T039: Verify positional arguments appear in transient and can be modified

## Checkpoint

User Story 3 complete - auto-generated commands work correctly

## Success Criteria

- SC-001: All `bd --help` commands have EIEIO classes and interactive commands

notes:
## User Story

A developer wants `beads-command-list` to render output in a tabulated-list buffer instead of terminal output. They override the rendering method for this class, and list commands display results in a structured buffer.

## Why P2 Priority

Different commands have different optimal output formats. Customization enables rich Emacs-native experiences.

## Independent Test

Can be tested by running `beads-list` and verifying output appears in a tabulated-list buffer with expected columns and data.

## Acceptance Scenarios

1. **Given** `beads-command-list` has custom rendering defined, **When** user executes the command, **Then** results appear in a tabulated-list buffer
2. **Given** default rendering is used, **When** user executes any command, **Then** output appears in the configured terminal mode (vterm/eat/term/compilation)
3. **Given** a command class defines custom execute behavior, **When** the command runs, **Then** the custom behavior is used instead of default

## Tasks

- T040: Verify existing `beads-list.el` uses custom tabulated-list rendering
- T041: [P] Document custom rendering pattern in quickstart.md
- T042: [P] Implement `beads-command-status` with custom buffer rendering
- T043: [P] Create `beads-status.el` transient
- T044: [P] Implement `beads-command-info` class
- T045: [P] Create `beads-info.el` transient
- T046: Verify default terminal rendering works for commands without custom rendering

## Checkpoint

User Story 4 complete - custom and default rendering both work

## Success Criteria

- SC-003: 100% of commands work with default behavior
title: US4: Customize Command Rendering

## User Story

A user wants to learn how to use beads.el commands. They open the Info manual and see exact text-based previews of each transient menu alongside descriptions of functionality.

## Why P2 Priority

Documentation with live previews ensures users always see accurate information, reducing confusion from stale docs.

## Independent Test

Can be tested by opening the Info manual and comparing transient previews to actual transient displays.

## Acceptance Scenarios

1. **Given** user opens beads.el Info manual, **When** viewing a command section, **Then** an exact text preview of the transient menu is shown
2. **Given** a transient menu changes, **When** the manual is regenerated, **Then** the preview in documentation matches the updated transient
3. **Given** user reads command documentation, **When** they try the actual command, **Then** the transient matches the documented preview exactly

## Tasks

- T047: Create `doc/beads.org` with standard Info manual structure
- T048: Add Introduction and Installation sections
- T049: [P] Add Main Menu (`M-x beads`) section with transient preview
- T050: [P] Add Create Command section with transient preview
- T051: [P] Add List Command section with transient preview

- T053: [P] Add Daemon Commands section
- T054: Create elisp function to generate transient preview text in `lisp/beads-doc.el`
- T055: Add Makefile target to compile `doc/beads.org` to Info format
- T056: Verify Info manual compiles without errors

## Checkpoint

User Story 5 complete - Info manual with previews is available

## User Story

A developer wants to verify command functionality works end-to-end. They run integration tests that execute commands against temporary beads/git repositories without polluting the working directory.

## Why P2 Priority

Integration tests ensure real functionality works, not just unit-level behavior. Temporary repos prevent test pollution.

## Independent Test

Can be tested by running the integration test suite and verifying all tests pass with temporary repos created and cleaned up.

## Acceptance Scenarios

1. **Given** integration tests are run, **When** a test executes a beads command, **Then** it operates on a temporary beads/git repository
2. **Given** integration tests complete, **When** checking the working directory, **Then** no test artifacts remain
3. **Given** a command's behavior changes, **When** running its integration test, **Then** the test validates the new behavior correctly

## Tasks

### Test Infrastructure
- T057: [P] Test `beads-test-with-temp-repo` creates and cleans up temp repo
- T058: [P] Test `beads-ready` command execution
- T059: [P] Test `beads-blocked` command execution
- T060: [P] Test `beads-daemon-list` command execution

### Implementation
- T061: Implement `beads-test-with-temp-repo` macro fully
- T062: Add integration test helper functions (create issue, setup test data)
- T063: [P] Write integration tests for `beads-create`
- T064: [P] Write integration tests for `beads-list`
- T065: [P] Write integration tests for `beads-show`
- T066: Verify `git status` shows no pollution after running integration tests

## Checkpoint

User Story 6 complete - integration tests run cleanly

## Success Criteria

- SC-004: 80%+ code coverage for command execution paths
- SC-005: Zero working directory pollution from tests

## User Story

A developer needs to verify real user interactions work correctly. They run Emacs inside tmux and manually test command workflows to ensure non-graphical compatibility.

## Why P3 Priority

Ensures beads.el works in terminal/server environments, not just GUI Emacs.

## Independent Test

Can be tested by launching Emacs in tmux, invoking commands, and verifying transients and output render correctly.

## Acceptance Scenarios

1. **Given** Emacs runs in terminal mode inside tmux, **When** user invokes `M-x beads`, **Then** the transient menu displays correctly
2. **Given** terminal Emacs with a command transient open, **When** user executes a command, **Then** output renders appropriately for terminal display

- T070: Verify command execution and output display in terminal mode
- T071: Test all terminal backends (compilation-mode fallback) in terminal Emacs

## Checkpoint

User Story 7 complete - terminal/tmux compatibility verified
